step.title: Step 4
step.actionDisplay: DOWN -> DOWN -> RIGHT -> DOWN -> RIGHT -> RIGHT

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 6 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: DOWN, DOWN, RIGHT, DOWN, RIGHT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 17, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Player moves 6 cells per action (not 3). Need to rebuild tile grid with 6x6 tiles. Player at (16,22) after 1 RIGHT from start (16,16).
- Goal model: Navigate from blue to green using the correct tile scale.
- Action model: Each action moves player 6 cells in the specified direction.
- Plan: Rebuild grid with 6x6 tiles, BFS to find shortest path, execute it.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 16 of about 19 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The player is STILL at (2, 2)! The boundary is [[27, 27], [27, 29], [28, 28], [29, 29], [29, 27]] which corresponds to the same position. The player didn't move at all during the last 6 actions!
Wait, but the animation_changed_bbox was [63, 44, 63, 50] - this is at row 63, which is near the bottom of the screen. This looks like a progress bar or timer that's changing, not the player moving.
This is very strange. The player is at tile (2, 2) with cell boundary around (27-29, 27-29). But the actions I executed were DOWN, DOWN, RIGHT, DOWN, RIGHT, RIGHT. The player should have moved!
Let me think about this differently. Maybe the player movement is not 6 cells per action. Let me reconsider.
Looking at the first move:
- Original player boundary: [[15, 15], [15, 17], [16, 16], [17, 17], [17, 15]]
- After RIGHT: [[15, 21], [15, 23], [16, 22], [17, 23], [17, 21]]
- Shift: +6 columns
So the first RIGHT moved the player 6 columns. But then the subsequent moves didn't move the player? That's very strange.
Wait, let me look at the animation_changed_bbox more carefully:
- First RIGHT: [15, 15, 63, 63] - large area
- Last batch: [63, 44, 63, 50] - small area at bottom
The first move had animation_frame_count=8 and animation_transition_count=6.
The last batch had animation_frame_count=6 and animation_transition_count=6.
Hmm, maybe the player is stuck at (2, 2) because it's blocked? Let me check the grid around (2, 2):
Row 2: BWbWBWBBBB
(2, 1) = W, (2, 2) = b, (2, 3) = W, (2, 4) = B
Row 1: BWWWWWBBBB
(1, 2) = W
Row 3: WWWWWWBBBB
(3, 2) = W
So the player can go UP to (1, 2), DOWN to (3, 2), LEFT to (2, 1), or RIGHT to (2, 3). All should be walkable.
But the player didn't move! Maybe the issue is that the player is not at tile (2, 2) in the game's internal state, even though visually it appears there. Or maybe the game has some other mechanic I'm not understanding.
Wait, let me reconsider. Maybe the player movement is cell-by-cell, not tile-by-tile. The first RIGHT moved 6 cells because the player was at the edge and needed to move to the next "room" or something. But subsequent moves might be 1 cell at a time.
Actually, let me look at the animation more carefully. The first RIGHT had:
- animation_frame_count: 8
- animation_transition_count: 6
- animation_changed_bbox: [15, 15, 63, 63]
This suggests there were 6 intermediate transitions, and the animation covered a large area. Maybe the player moved 6 cells, one at a time, with intermediate frames.
But then the subsequent batch had:
- animation_frame_count: 6
- animation_transition_count: 6
- animation_changed_bbox: [63, 44, 63, 50]
This is a small area at the bottom (row 63). This doesn't look like player movement at all. It looks like a progress bar.
Wait, maybe the actions are being recorded but not actually moving the player because the player is in a different state. Or maybe the game requires some other interaction.
Let me try a single action and see what happens.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: Player didn't move! Still at (2, 2). The animation was at the bottom (row 63) - looks like a progress bar. Let me try a single action to understand what's happening.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try a single DOWN action to see what happens
result = action(['DOWN'])
print(f"Result: {last_action_result}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'b':
        print(f"Player after DOWN: boundary={n['boundary']}")
        break
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
